Public schools adapted the inherited folk-football impulse into formalized games, each writing its own code from the same raw tradition.
The incompatibility between competing public-school codes — especially over handling the ball — created the direct need that the 1863 FA meeting resolved with a single rulebook.
A widespread popular game existed to be codified; without an established folk practice there was nothing for the FA to standardize.
Standard rules made organized competitive matches possible, which in turn created clubs willing to pay for the best players — the basis for legalized professionalism.
A shared rulebook was the precondition for a regular fixture list between clubs; you cannot run a league without agreed laws.
Paid professional players needed regular, revenue-generating fixtures to be financially sustainable, directly motivating the founding of a scheduled league.
The Football League established the gate-and-fixture commercial template that later international competitions and FIFA scaled up commercially.
A single portable rulebook let British traders and sailors teach the same game everywhere they went; an un-codified folk game could not have spread as a consistent sport.
Once multiple nations played the same codified game, a body to govern matches between them became necessary — the explicit purpose for which FIFA was founded.
FIFA adopted the FA's Laws of the Game as its basis; the international body presupposed an existing shared code to govern.
The organizational model of a governing association running structured competition informed how international football was institutionalized.
FIFA provided the international governance that let an Olympic football tournament be run as a sanctioned competition between national associations.
The first World Cup was organized and run by FIFA; the world championship existed only because an international governing body existed to stage it.
The popularity of Olympic football, and its amateur-only restriction, demonstrated demand for a professional world championship and hastened FIFA's decision to launch one.
A genuine world championship required competitive national teams on multiple continents, which the earlier spread of the game had produced — Uruguay, Argentina, and Brazil were already strong.
Once a recurring world championship existed, host nations could appropriate it; Italy 1934 was the first to weaponize hosting for state propaganda.
The Maracanazo's national trauma was only possible because the World Cup had become the supreme prize whose loss could wound a nation.
The early demonstration that hosting projects national power shaped FIFA's later willingness to award tournaments strategically across regions.
A regular, marquee international tournament gave early television a premium live event worth broadcasting; the World Cup format was the content TV needed.
Television broadcasting of the World Cup established the audience and demand that justified investing in live satellite transmission a generation later.
Worldwide simultaneous satellite broadcast created a global advertising surface, which is precisely what made large multinational sponsorship deals valuable to FIFA.
Televised reach turned matches into marketable media, the precondition for brands paying to be associated with the tournament.
Havelange explicitly pursued corporate sponsorship and TV-rights revenue, signing the Coca-Cola and Adidas partnerships that built FIFA's commercial base.
Havelange won the presidency by promising more World Cup places to African and Asian federations and delivered the first expansion to 24 teams.
The globalized broadcast reach of the 1970 tournament made FIFA a commercially valuable property, the platform Havelange built his commercial presidency upon.
Guaranteed sponsorship and broadcast revenue gave FIFA the financial confidence to enlarge the tournament and absorb the added logistical cost.
The 1982 expansion established the precedent and political logic — more slots for under-represented confederations — that the 2026 jump to 48 teams extended.
A larger, more global tournament increased FIFA's appetite to enter major untapped markets like the United States.
FIFA awarded 1994 to the USA explicitly to open the lucrative American consumer and sponsorship market, the logical extension of its commercialization strategy.
Launching a domestic professional league was a condition of the US 1994 bid; MLS was founded in 1996 as a direct consequence.
Two decades of MLS built the stadiums, audiences, and football infrastructure that made the United States a credible anchor for the 2026 bid.
The commercial and organizational success of USA 1994 demonstrated North America could stage and monetize the tournament, paving the way for the 2026 award.
A professional labour market for players had to exist before a court could rule on the legality of restricting their movement; Bosman acted on a century-old professional system.
By freeing player movement and removing EU foreigner limits, Bosman triggered wage inflation and multinational squad-building — the galáctico star economy.
Champions League TV and sponsorship money gave elite clubs the budgets to assemble squads of global superstars, defining the galáctico era.
Global live broadcast made club competitions valuable enough to repackage as a premium, sponsor-funded Champions League product.
Decades of player globalization built worldwide fan attachment to stars and leagues, broadening the global audience the 2026 tournament is sized to serve.
Havelange's drive to take the tournament beyond Europe and the Americas set the policy direction that produced the first Asian World Cup.
The success of the first Asian and first co-hosted tournament reinforced FIFA's rotation policy of awarding hosting to new regions, including Africa.
Korea/Japan 2002 proved that splitting a World Cup across countries was logistically workable, providing the template the tri-nation 2026 model extended to three hosts.
FIFA's rotation logic and the global-hosting norm made a return of the tournament to North America in 2026 fit an established pattern of spreading hosting worldwide.
Multi-angle broadcast replays publicly exposed officiating errors, building the pressure that led FIFA to admit goal-line technology into the laws.
Accepting goal-line technology broke FIFA's resistance to in-game tech and directly opened the path to the more comprehensive video assistant referee system.
Tech-mediated, replay-rich officiating suited the interactive digital broadcast experience that streaming platforms monetize.
The decades-long television rights model established the media-rights revenue framework that streaming platforms later inherited and extended.
The vast media-rights and sponsorship money flowing through FIFA created the very incentives for the bribery the 2015 indictments exposed.
Havelange's commercialization concentrated enormous revenue and discretionary power in FIFA, the structural conditions in which the later corruption flourished.
The scandal forced out Sepp Blatter and triggered the election Infantino won on a reform-and-expansion platform.
Infantino championed the 48-team format to grow revenue and secure votes from smaller federations; the 2017 expansion was the centerpiece of his agenda.
Post-scandal reforms produced a more transparent, member-vote host-selection process that favored the low-risk, infrastructure-rich United bid.
A 48-team, 104-match tournament demanded a host with abundant existing stadiums and transport, which is exactly why the multi-country United bid was chosen.
The sheer scale of 48 teams made spreading matches across three countries the practical way to stage the tournament.
The winning bid was itself a three-nation proposal, so the 2026 tri-nation hosting model is the direct realization of that bid.
Winning the 2018 vote awarded the USA, Canada, and Mexico the right to stage the 2026 World Cup.
The 2017 decision is what makes 2026 the first 48-team World Cup; the tournament's defining format comes directly from it.
The agreed tri-nation logistics framework is the operational basis on which the 2026 tournament is staged.
Mexico's proven hosting infrastructure from 1970 and 1986 made it a low-risk co-host, strengthening the bid that delivered 2026.
Mexico's inclusion in the winning United bid is what makes it a three-time World Cup host in 2026.
Mexico's landmark 1970 tournament built the stadium and broadcast heritage that underpins its standing as a repeat host into 2026.
The women's game's commercial breakthrough widened football's global audience and sponsorship base, part of the growth the expanded 2026 tournament is built to capture.
Streaming-era media rights broaden global access and revenue, helping fund and distribute the larger 2026 tournament.
The Champions League globalized year-round club fandom, sustaining the worldwide football audience the 2026 World Cup addresses.
The enduring understanding of hosting as national soft power motivates nations — including the 2026 co-hosts — to invest in winning bids.
France's clinical 3–0 knockout opener restored the holders' rhythm and a clean-sheet base they carried into a tight win over Paraguay.
France and Morocco both winning on July 4 set up the Morocco–France quarter-final — two concurrent R16 results that together framed the last-eight tie, not one causing the other.
Brazil’s laboured 2–1 over Japan exposed a shaky defence and framed the Norway tie as a genuine upset opportunity rather than a formality.
Mexico’s composed 2–0 over Ecuador secured the Round-of-16 place and set up a home tie with England, keeping the co-host story alive into the knockouts.
Canada’s home exit echoes the World Cup’s oldest host-heartbreak template — the 1950 Maracanazo — where the tournament’s stakes are measured in a host nation’s collective memory.
The 2017 vote to expand to 48 teams reshaped the knockout bracket; ties like Mexico–England sit inside the enlarged, redrawn path that expansion produced.
Mexico’s deep hosting heritage — a third World Cup after 1970 and 1986 — is the backdrop to a co-host advancing on home soil in 2026.
Decades of globalized player development — Morocco’s squad drawn from Europe’s top leagues — underpin a non-traditional power reaching consecutive World Cup quarter-finals.
Mexico’s confident 2–0 opening-night win banked early points and belief that carried into a controlled second-match win over South Korea.
Morocco's 3–0 win banked a goal-difference cushion heading into Morocco vs France.
